request.question: Which film based on a novel written by Mary Hayley Bell in 1959, had cast member Ashley Lloyd?

title: Juliet Mills
Crispian Mills
Hayley Mills
Whistle Down the Wind (musical)
Whistle Down the Wind (novel)
Mary Hayley Bell
Ashley Lloyd
Sky West and Crooked
Whistle Down the Wind (film)
Sir John Mills' Moving Memories
sentences: Juliet Maryon Mills (born 21 November 1941) is a British and American actress.
She is the daughter of actor Sir John Mills and Mary Hayley Bell and the eldest of three siblings: her younger siblings are actress Hayley Mills and director Jonathan Mills.
Crispian Mills (born 18 January 1973 as Crispian John David Boulting), also known as Krishna Kantha Das, is an English singer-songwriter, guitarist, and film director.

He is the son of actress Hayley Mills and director Roy Boulting, the grandson of Sir John Mills and Mary Hayley Bell, nephew of Juliet Mills and director Jonathan Mills, and half-brother to Jason Lawson.
Hayley Catherine Rose Vivien Mills (born 18 April 1946) is an English actress.

The daughter of Sir John Mills and Mary Hayley Bell, and younger sister of actress Juliet Mills, Mills began her acting career as a child and was hailed as a promising newcomer, winning the BAFTA Award for Most Promising Newcomer for her performance in the British crime drama film "Tiger Bay" (1959), the Academy Juvenile Award for Disney's "Pollyanna" (1960) and Golden Globe Award for New Star of the Year – Actress in 1961.
During her early career, she appeared in six films for Walt Disney, including her dual role as twins Susan and Sharon in the Disney film "The Parent Trap" (1961).
Her performance in "Whistle Down the Wind" (a 1961 adaptation of the novel written by her mother) saw Mills nominated for BAFTA Award for Best British Actress.
Whistle Down the Wind is a musical with music composed by Andrew Lloyd Webber, who also co-wrote its book with Patricia Knop and Gale Edwards, and its lyrics were written by Jim Steinman.
It is based on the 1961 film "Whistle Down the Wind", whose source novel of the same name was written by Mary Hayley Bell in 1959.
Whistle Down the Wind was a novella written by Mary Hayley Bell and illustrated by Owen Edwards.
It was published in 1959 by Boardman and retailed for 12 shillings and sixpence (62½p).
The central characters are three children — Swallow, Brat and Poor Baby.
The story of the Great Secret is told by Brat.
Mary Hayley Bell, Lady Mills (22 January 1911 – 1 December 2005) was an English actress and writer, married for 64 years to actor Sir John Mills.
Her novel "Whistle Down the Wind" was adapted as a film, starring her teenaged daughter, actress Hayley Mills.

Ashley Lloyd (billed as Ashley Luke Lloyd) (born (1991--)6 1991 ) is a British actor and dancer, who has performed in hit musical theatre shows such as "Billy Elliot the Musical" and "Whistle Down the Wind".
In 2012 Lloyd appeared in the new musical "Loserville" at the Garrick Theatre in London.
Lloyd is now appearing in the Original London Cast of Dreamgirls at The Savoy Theatre
Sky West and Crooked (known in the United States as Gypsy Girl) is a 1965 romantic drama film, featuring actress Hayley Mills.
The film was directed by her father, John Mills, and was co-written by her mother, Mary Hayley Bell.
Whistle Down the Wind is a 1961 British film, directed by Bryan Forbes and with screenplay by Keith Waterhouse and Willis Hall.
The film is based on the 1959 novel of the same name by Mary Hayley Bell and stars her daughter, Hayley Mills.
Sir John Mills' Moving Memories is a British documentary film featuring 16mm color home movies shot by the actor Sir John Mills.
It documents his life between 1946 and 1969, directed and edited by Marcus Dillistone and produced by his son Jonathan Mills.
Commentary was provided by Sir John (who was over 90 years old at the time), Hayley Mills, Juliet Mills and Sir Richard Attenborough.
His wife Mary Hayley Bell is also seen towards the end of the film listening to her husband singing at the piano.
The scene was later to be screened in full when Sir John appeared on the Parkinson show.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.summary_1:
Facts from passages:
- [4] "Whistle Down the Wind" — novel published in 1959 (paraphrase: "published in 1959").
- [2] Whistle Down the Wind (film) — "a 1961 British film" and "stars ... Hayley Mills" (short quoted fragment: "a 1961 British film").
- [1] Whistle Down the Wind (musical) — musical "is based on the 1961 film" whose source novel "was written by Mary Hayley Bell in 1959" (short quoted fragment: "was written by Mary Hayley Bell in 1959").
- [7] There is also a "Whistle Down the Wind (1989 musical)" adaptation (paraphrase: musical adaptation exists).
- [3] Mary Hayley Bell page confirms her novel "Whistle Down the Wind" was adapted as a film (paraphrase).

Bridge entities and ambiguities:
- Bridge entity: the novel "Whistle Down the Wind" (1959) connects to multiple adaptations present in passages: 
  - Whistle Down the Wind (1961 film) [2] — a film.
  - Whistle Down the Wind (musical) [1] and Whistle Down the Wind (1989 musical) [7] — stage musicals.
  Marked ambiguous: the question asks "Which film ... had cast member Ashley Lloyd?" but multiple adaptation types exist (film vs musicals) and the passages do not mention Ashley Lloyd at all.

Missing/needed facts (preventing final answer):
- Whether Ashley Lloyd appears in the 1961 film or any other film adaptation of the 1959 novel.
- Ashley Lloyd's filmography / credits to link him to a specific adaptation.
- Cast lists for relevant adaptations (1961 film and any later film or filmed stage versions) to confirm his involvement.

Prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve ambiguity:
1) Fetch the Ashley Lloyd biographical/credits page (title: "Ashley Lloyd") — extract the "Filmography" or "Credits" section (exact field: list of film and stage roles; look for any entry titled "Whistle Down the Wind" or a film explicitly credited as adaptation of Mary Hayley Bell's novel).
2) Fetch "Whistle Down the Wind (film)" full page — extract the "Cast" section (exact keyword: "Cast") to confirm whether Ashley Lloyd is listed for the 1961 film.
3) Fetch "Whistle Down the Wind (musical)" (Andrew Lloyd Webber) full page and production-specific pages (e.g., original West End, tours) — extract "Cast" or "Productions" sections and search for "Ashley Lloyd".
4) Fetch "Whistle Down the Wind (1989 musical)" page — extract "Cast" / production credits for any filmed productions listing Ashley Lloyd.
5) If Ashley Lloyd's page or casts indicate a different film adaptation title, fetch that specific film page (title as indicated) and extract its "Cast" and "Release/Adaptation" info.

No final answer provided because Ashley Lloyd's association with any film/adaptation named in the passages is not present or unambiguous in the supplied material.
response.hop2_query: Ashley Lloyd credits Whistle Down the Wind film
hop2_docs: Ashley Lloyd | Ashley Lloyd (billed as Ashley Luke Lloyd) (born (1991--)6 1991 ) is a British actor and dancer, who has performed in hit musical theatre shows such as "Billy Elliot the Musical" and "Whistle Down the Wind".  In 2012 Lloyd appeared in the new musical "Loserville" at the Garrick Theatre in London.  Lloyd is now appearing in the Original London Cast of Dreamgirls at The Savoy Theatre

Whistle Down the Wind (musical) | Whistle Down the Wind is a musical with music composed by Andrew Lloyd Webber, who also co-wrote its book with Patricia Knop and Gale Edwards, and its lyrics were written by Jim Steinman.  It is based on the 1961 film "Whistle Down the Wind", whose source novel of the same name was written by Mary Hayley Bell in 1959.

Whistle Down the Wind (film) | Whistle Down the Wind is a 1961 British film, directed by Bryan Forbes and with screenplay by Keith Waterhouse and Willis Hall.  The film is based on the 1959 novel of the same name by Mary Hayley Bell and stars her daughter, Hayley Mills.
Whistle Down the Wind (1989 musical) | Whistle Down the Wind is a musical based on the novel by Mary Hayley Bell, adapted by Richard Taylor and Russell Labey, with music and lyrics by Richard Taylor.

No Matter What (Boyzone song) | "No Matter What" is a song from the 1996 musical "Whistle Down the Wind" and popularised by Irish boyband Boyzone in 1998 when they recorded it to tie in with the show's first UK production.  The recording was written and produced by Andrew Lloyd Webber, Jim Steinman, Nigel Wright with additional production by Franglen & Lupino.  The song was also featured on the US edition of the soundtrack to the 1999 film "Notting Hill", and was released to American radio on 10 May 1999.

Whistle Down the Wind (novel) | Whistle Down the Wind was a novella written by Mary Hayley Bell and illustrated by Owen Edwards.  It was published in 1959 by Boardman and retailed for 12 shillings and sixpence (62½p).  The central characters are three children — Swallow, Brat and Poor Baby.  The story of the Great Secret is told by Brat.

A Timeless Evening with Sarah Brightman | A Timeless Evening With Sarah Brightman was the first concert tour by English soprano Sarah Brightman accompanied by the London Symphony Orchestra.  The repertoire performed by Brightman was a balanced combination between well-known Classical and Broadway pieces by composers such as Puccini, Gershwin, Delibes, Bernstein and Andrew Lloyd Webber.  Lloyd Webber joined Brightman on stage to perform together the song "Whistle Down the Wind".  Italian tenor Andrea Bocelli sang "Time to Say Goodbye" in a duet with Brightman.

response.summary_2: Ashley Lloyd is credited with performing in the stage musical "Whistle Down the Wind." Whistle Down the Wind (film) is a 1961 British film based on Mary Hayley Bell's 1959 novel.
response.answer: None — the summaries show Ashley Lloyd performed in the stage musical Whistle Down the Wind, not in the 1961 film.
